condensed_reasoning:
## Research

I investigated whether Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi would visit South Korea before September 1, 2026. My primary tool was a `research` sub-agent, which — after its own `asknews` calls failed with 403 credit-overage errors — recovered via a series of `WebSearch` queries and a `WebFetch` of the key Korea Times article. I built a comprehensive picture of the visit's status and the surrounding diplomatic context.

## Key Evidence

- **Visit stalled with no rescheduled date.** As of the most authoritative reporting (**Korea Times, April 20, 2026**), South Korea's MOFA said **"no specific arrangements have been finalized"** and discussions had not progressed to concrete date-setting.
- **The causative Taiwan-labeling dispute.** South Korea's e-arrival card had listed Taiwan as "Taiwan (China)"/"China (Taiwan)"; after Taiwan objected, Seoul removed the departure/destination fields entirely on **March 31, 2026**, prompting China to object (**April 14 MFA statement** calling the labeling "entirely natural"). China reportedly put the visit on hold and also blocked a South Korean NIS chief visit (UPI, April 21).
- **Wang Yi's travel priorities lay elsewhere.** He visited Pyongyang April 9–10, accompanied Xi's DPRK state visit June 8–9, and his confirmed early-July travel was to the **Nordic countries (July 2–8)** — not Seoul.
- **Offsetting reset dynamics.** China–ROK ties are in a "reset" phase: Lee Jae-myung's Jan 5, 2026 Beijing summit produced **15 agreements**, and Seoul actively wants the visit. The key catalyst — APEC in Gyeongju — falls Oct 31–Nov 1, **outside** the window.

**Data-vintage caveat:** My most detailed reporting was ~3 months stale (April 2026), with no May–July confirmation of rescheduling or cancellation.

## Conclusion

I anchored on the base rate that a diplomatic visit described as "stalled/postponed with no rescheduled date" materializes within a specific 2-month window only ~15–20% of the time. Via a `reflection` tool I assembled five weighted factors (four negative: stalled status, unresolved dispute, competing NK/Nordic focus, short unscheduled window; one positive: the reset phase and Seoul's desire), summing to a logit of **-1.8**. A `premortem` check constructed the strong counterargument (reset momentum, dispute defused March 31, possible trilateral FM meeting) but approved the estimate as calibrated. I concluded the status quo resolves **NO**, submitting a probability of **0.14 (14%)**.

capability_gaps: A diplomatic-calendar tool to check for scheduled trilateral (China-Japan-Korea) or bilateral FM meetings in a given window — the agent explicitly noted this would have helped pin down whether a structural event falls in Jul-Aug 2026.
subtle_bugs: Multiple WebSearch queries returned near-identical April-2026 article sets, giving an impression of corroboration when in fact the same underlying reporting was being re-surfaced; no genuinely fresh May-July data existed.

classification.approach: Qualitative status-quo assessment anchored on a base rate for a 'stalled/no-date' diplomatic visit materializing within a specific 2-month window, adjusted by five weighted logit factors.
classification.key_uncertainty: Whether the stalled Wang Yi visit or a China-Japan-Korea trilateral FM meeting gets scheduled into the Jul-Aug window, given that the latest hard reporting is ~3 months stale (April 2026).

risk_flags.evidence: The decisive scheduling evidence (Korea MOFA 'no arrangements finalized') is from April 20, 2026 — roughly 11 weeks before the July 9 forecast date and covering only ~3 weeks of the 8-week resolution window. AskNews failed (403), and WebSearch surfaced no May-July confirmation. The agent flagged this honestly and lowered factor confidences, so it is a minor rather than major flag.

The agent never checked the diplomatic-summit calendar for a China-Japan-Korea trilateral foreign ministers' meeting — which its own premortem identified as the single most plausible YES catalyst in the window. It noted the capability gap but did not attempt to resolve it (e.g., searching for '2026 trilateral foreign ministers meeting Korea host'). South Korea was the expected 2026 trilateral host, so this could have materially informed the estimate.

strengths: Correctly identified the status-quo dynamic: a visit explicitly reported as 'on hold, no date' rarely materializes in a narrow 2-month window, and anchored on an appropriate ~15-20% base rate.
Precisely engaged the resolution criteria — correct FM (Wang Yi), correct Jul 1-Sep 1 window — and correctly excluded the APEC Gyeongju catalyst as falling outside the window (Oct 31-Nov 1).
Gracefully recovered from AskNews 403 failures by pivoting to WebSearch and a targeted WebFetch of the primary Korea Times source, rather than abandoning the research.
Honestly surfaced the ~3-month data-vintage gap and propagated it into factor confidences (0.6-0.8) rather than treating April reporting as current.
Premortem produced a genuine, evidence-specified counterargument (reset phase, dispute defused March 31, trilateral possibility) and named concrete facts that would flip it to 60%+.
weaknesses: Did not attempt to resolve its own identified key uncertainty — whether a China-Japan-Korea trilateral FM meeting (which South Korea was expected to host in 2026) falls in the Jul-Aug window — despite this being the main YES pathway.
Factors 1 and 2 (stalled visit / Taiwan friction) share thematic roots; while the premortem judged this acceptable, there is mild risk of double-counting the same underlying negative signal.
Relied on a single research sub-agent call with no fresh follow-up targeting May-July specifically beyond generic queries that all returned the same April-vintage articles.

issues: AskNews MCP returned 403015 'maximum billable overage limit for credits' on both attempts inside the research sub-agent — a recurring billing/quota problem that wastes calls, though here it was recovered via WebSearch.
ToolSearch returned an empty result for the 'news search recent events' query, providing no value.
